findings.description: range(session.total_chunks - 1) skips the final chunk, so every multi-chunk upload is silently truncated by one chunk.
dest_path is built from unsanitized session.filename, so '../' or an absolute path enables path traversal / arbitrary file write outside UPLOAD_DIR.
Chunk path is built from unsanitized upload_id, allowing chunk writes outside CHUNK_DIR via traversal characters.
Chunks are written with no per-chunk or cumulative size limit; MAX_FILE_MB is never enforced on ingest, permitting disk-exhaustion DoS.
Two uploads with the same filename overwrite the earlier stored file because dest_path uses the raw filename with no uniqueness.
verify_checksum reads the entire file into memory with f.read() instead of streaming, spiking memory for files up to/over 200MB.
assemble never calls verify_checksum or file_size_ok, so the module's advertised 'verify' step is not enforced in the store path.
The _sessions dict and per-session received set are mutated across methods with no locking, racing under concurrent uploads.
start() overwrites any existing in-progress session for the same upload_id, orphaning its already-written chunk files.
Incomplete sessions that are never assembled or aborted leave chunk files on disk forever with no TTL or expiry cleanup.
Only total_chunks < 1 is checked; no upper bound allows a caller to request a huge chunk count for resource exhaustion.
Checksum comparison is case-sensitive, so a valid uppercase-hex client digest would falsely mismatch the lowercase hexdigest() output.
No exception handling: a missing or unreadable chunk leaves a partially written file in UPLOAD_DIR and inconsistent session state.
os.makedirs uses default permissions, so upload/chunk directories may be world-accessible depending on umask.
TOCTOU race between os.path.exists and os.remove in _cleanup_chunks (minor; remove could fail if file vanishes).
Digest is compared with a non-constant-time != operator, though for a client-supplied integrity hash this is largely a non-issue.
Uses Optional[str] while the rest of the file uses PEP 585 builtins (set[int], dict[str, ...]), an inconsistent typing style.
